original_reasoning: مقاربة الخطا والكلام carries a core of closeness and sticking-together that plausibly matches sticky or adhesive.
هيمن=dominate/oversee (Mohaymin); ἡγεμόνευεν=led/governed (from ἡγεμών=leader). Strong semantic match via authority/leadership

أقعث: أجزلها العطية (made the gift generous/abundant). κατατίθημι aorist=having placed down, deposited, laid down (also: having bestowed). Both describe the act of giving/bestowing something. Generous giving (إجزال العطية) parallels depositing/bestowing (κατα-τίθημι).
Arabic denotes slipperiness and sharp edge; Greek is 'dialect'. Sharp tongue and articulate speech give a plausible chain from physical edge to linguistic form.

الأشقر includes 'من الدم ما صار علقاً' (blood that became clotted). γλίσχρος=sticky, viscous, adhesive. Clotted blood IS sticky/viscous. This is a direct material-property match: coagulated blood shares the exact physical quality described by γλίσχρος.

Arabic khamad = subside, become low/extinguished (fire). Greek chamadis = 'to the ground, downward' (from chamai 'on the ground'). Both share the 'going down/low' spatial-figurative core. Strong concrete chain — fire dying down meets 'to the ground'. Note phonetic similarity khamad/chamadis.

Arabic خندريس=name of (old) wine, also = old wheat/groats in classical sources (Lane). Greek khondros=groats, grain, lump (esp. of wheat/barley). The Arabic word is widely cited as a Greek loan precisely from khondros — semantic field of grain/groats matches even if the masadiq line shows only the wine sense. Strong masadiq match via the wheat meaning.

Arabic الأسكتان: labia (skin folds at sides of vulva). Greek σκύτινος: made of leather/skin. Both share the semantic field of 'skin/hide material'. The Arabic relates to skin-like anatomical folds; σκύτος = skin. Plausible semantic drift.
